planting.note: Sow in early spring around the last frost date, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first frost to allow fall establishment.

directions: Inoculate seed with Medicago-specific rhizobium and broadcast at 15–20 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft), raking seed to 1/4 in depth into a firm, well-prepared seedbed with pH 6.5–7.0. Alfalfa fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre per year once established and deep taproots (up to 6 ft) break hardpan and mine subsoil minerals. Terminate by mowing at early bloom stage — typically every 30–40 days in summer — before the first killing frost; turning it under releases a large pulse of nitrogen. In perennial plantings, allow one cutting to go to seed to rejuvenate the stand every two or three years.

planting.note: Frost-seed onto bare or thin ground in late winter to early spring; the freeze-thaw cycle works seed into the soil surface naturally.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 1/4–1/2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a lightly raked surface; the tiny seed needs good soil contact but should not be covered more than 1/4 in. White clover spreads by stolons to form a dense living mulch that fixes nitrogen and feeds beneficial insects with nectar-rich flowers. Mow periodically to prevent flowering from shading out adjacent crops, or allow it to bloom freely when used as a pollinator corridor. In annual vegetable beds, turn the stand under in early spring and wait 2–3 weeks before planting so the nitrogen flush is available to the next crop.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or frost-seed in late winter; short-lived perennial that fixes nitrogen and blooms its second year.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed onto a prepared bed at 8–10 lb per acre, raking to 1/4 in depth; red clover tolerates slightly acidic soils and moderate shade better than most legume cover crops. It overwinters in zones 4 and warmer and puts on vigorous growth in early spring, often topping 2 ft before bloom. Terminate at early flower stage — before the first florets open to brown — to capture maximum fixed nitrogen of 80–150 lb per acre. A second flush of growth often follows mowing, allowing a second cut of biomass before final incorporation.

planting.note: Sow in early fall 4–8 weeks before first frost, or in early spring; survives light frost but not hard freezes, so it winter-kills in zones 6 and colder.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb per acre, raking seed lightly to 1/4 in depth; berseem germinates in near-freezing soil and establishes faster than crimson clover. Unlike most clovers, berseem regrows vigorously after each mowing, allowing two to three cuts before termination, with each cut releasing a pulse of nitrogen. It tolerates moderate salinity and poorly drained soils better than most cool-season legumes, making it useful in difficult garden sites. Terminate by incorporating the last flush of growth 3–4 weeks before planting the next crop.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover-crop legumes.

directions: Broadcast or drill inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 25 sq ft), 1/2 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; cowpeas germinate and grow rapidly, fixing 100–150 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing summer weeds with their spreading vines. The crop tolerates sandy, infertile, and slightly acidic soils where most legumes struggle. Terminate by mowing at early pod stage before seeds mature to prevent volunteer plants; large amounts of vine biomass decompose quickly in warm weather. Pairs well with sorghum-sudangrass for a combined legume-grass summer cover.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before last spring frost, or in early fall in mild climates; flowers arrive in about 6 weeks and are among the best for attracting bees.

directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 3–5 lb per acre (a light dusting per 100 sq ft) on the surface of a firm, moist seedbed without covering, as seed needs light to germinate; rake in very lightly if birds are a problem. Phacelia establishes quickly in cool soil, outcompeting many weeds with dense, ferny foliage that also conserves soil moisture. The blue-purple flowers are exceptional for beneficial insects and bees — allow partial bloom before mowing to maximize pollinator benefit. Terminate by mowing just as the last flowers fade; it decomposes rapidly and leaves no allelopathic residue.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; a fast-growing brassica smother crop with biofumigant properties.

directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a firm seedbed, raking to 1/4 in depth; mustard germinates in cold soil within days and establishes an aggressive canopy that smothers weeds. When chopped and incorporated at early flower stage, the glucosinolates in the tissue break down into biofumigant compounds that suppress soil-borne pathogens and weed seeds — a technique called biofumigation. Mow and immediately till to a 4–6 in depth, then water and tarp the bed for 2 weeks to trap fumigant gases. Terminate before it sets seed, as mustard reseeds abundantly and can become a weed.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before first fall frost; germinates and establishes faster than perennial ryegrass and can be interseeded into standing late-season crops.

directions: Broadcast seed at 20–25 lb per acre (about 1/2 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a prepared surface and rake to 1/4 in depth; annual ryegrass has one of the fastest establishment rates of all cool-season grasses, providing rapid erosion control and weed suppression. The dense, fibrous root system scavenges residual nitrogen effectively, locking it up over winter to prevent leaching. Terminate in early spring before it joints and sets seed — once headed, it becomes very difficult to kill without herbicide or repeated tillage. Mow to 2–3 in and till, or use a roller-crimper at boot stage for no-till termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to break down before planting.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; oats winter-kill below about 10°F, leaving a ready-to-plant mat of residue in spring without tillage.

directions: Broadcast seed at 50–80 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 30 sq ft) and rake to 1/2 in depth; oats establish quickly and produce substantial biomass before being killed by hard freezes. Their winter-killed residue lies flat as a weed-suppressing mulch that is easy to plant or transplant through in spring without additional termination work. Often mixed with field peas or hairy vetch to add nitrogen to the primarily carbonaceous grass residue. In mild-winter regions (zones 8–9), oats may overwinter and require mowing to terminate before spring planting.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; common vetch is slightly more cold-tolerant than crimson clover and grows in poorer soils than hairy vetch.

directions: Sow inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 20 sq ft) in fall, raking to 1/2 in depth; common vetch tolerates sandy, low-fertility soils where hairy vetch may struggle and establishes a climbing, vining habit that benefits from a grass companion for support. It fixes 80–120 lb of nitrogen per acre over winter and spring and is easier to terminate than hairy vetch because the vines are thinner and dry down faster. Mow at early bloom before pods swell to prevent reseeding; the narrow tendriled stems break down readily and release nitrogen within 2–3 weeks.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost date in spring; quinoa bolts in midsummer heat, so timing for a cool finish is critical in most climates.

directions: Broadcast or sow seed 1/4 in deep in firm, well-drained soil and thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 3–4 in tall; quinoa is highly drought-tolerant once established and prefers cool, dry weather through seed fill. The colorful seed heads (red, orange, or cream) are ready to harvest when the seed falls easily from the panicle when rubbed — typically after the first light fall frost has dried the plant. Cut the seed heads into paper bags, dry for 1–2 weeks in a warm airy space, then thresh by rubbing heads between hands and winnow away the chaff outdoors in a breeze. Rinse seed thoroughly before eating to remove bitter saponins from the seed coat.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; grain amaranth thrives in hot weather and tolerates drought and poor soil better than most grains.

directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seed 1/8 in deep, then thin to 12–18 in when seedlings are 2 in tall; amaranth seed is tiny and germinates readily in warm soil but competing weeds must be controlled in the first 3 weeks before the plant takes over. The tall, dramatic seed heads ripen from the bottom up — harvest when the top of the panicle begins to dry and seed crumbles easily when pinched. Cut heads into a bag or over a tarp, dry for 1–2 weeks, then thresh and winnow; a single plant can yield 1/4–1/2 lb of seed. Grain amaranth seed is gluten-free and high in lysine, making it nutritionally complementary to corn or wheat.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is reliably above 60°F; grain sorghum needs a long, warm season but withstands drought far better than corn.

directions: Drill seed 1/2 in deep in rows 24–30 in apart, thinning to 6–8 in within the row once plants are established; grain sorghum stalls visibly during drought but resumes growth when moisture returns, unlike corn which suffers permanent yield loss. The compact seed heads (panicles) are ready to harvest when seeds dent and the plant begins to dry down naturally, roughly 30 days after bloom. Cut heads by hand into bags or combine at low cylinder speed to avoid shattering; dry grain to 13% moisture or less before storage. Whole sorghum grain is gluten-free and can be popped like popcorn or ground into flour.

planting.note: Sow after last frost; proso millet is the shortest-season grain available to home gardeners, maturing in as little as 60 days.

directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep at roughly 20–25 lb per acre in home-scale rows and thin to 4–6 in; proso millet germinates quickly in warm soil and produces an open panicle of seed in just 60 days, making it practical even in short-season gardens above zone 4. Harvest entire stalks when the lower seeds are hard and the panicle begins to droop; hang bundles upside-down over a tarp for 2 weeks to finish drying. Thresh by beating bundles against the inside of a barrel and winnow outdoors; the hull is loose and comes free with light rubbing in warm water. Proso millet is gluten-free and has the lowest water requirement of any grain crop.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; finger millet thrives in hot, humid conditions and tolerates mildly acidic or infertile soils.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in prepared rows or broadcast on a firm seedbed, thinning to 6–9 in when plants are 4 in tall; finger millet produces distinctive finger-like seed head rays that are diagnostic and easy to recognize at harvest. The seed turns dark reddish-brown when ripe and the heads curve inward; harvest before birds strip them by cutting the heads at early dough stage and finishing the dry-down on a tarp in the sun. Rub heads between palms to release the very small seeds, which need no hulling; grind into flour (ragi flour) or soak and ferment for traditional beverages. The crop is notably high in calcium and iron compared to other small grains.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is warm; teff tolerates both waterlogged and drought conditions better than most grain crops.

directions: Surface-broadcast seed — it is extremely small (poppy-seed sized) and must not be buried; mix seed with sand to achieve even distribution at roughly 2–3 lb per 1,000 sq ft, then press lightly with a roller or board for good soil contact. Teff tillers prolifically and forms a graceful, open canopy with hundreds of fine panicles per plant; harvest the entire plant at the base when the majority of seeds have turned brown but before they shatter in a breeze. Thresh by beating bundles over a smooth tarp and winnow carefully in a gentle airflow — the seed is light and easily blown away. Teff grain is naturally gluten-free and is the basis for Ethiopian injera flatbread.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before the first fall frost to allow establishment before winter; einkorn overwinters as a low rosette and resumes growth in early spring.

directions: Sow seed 1 in deep in fall at 80–100 lb per acre (about 2–3 oz per 100 sq ft); einkorn is an ancient hulled wheat that outperforms modern wheats on poor, low-fertility soils and has exceptional drought tolerance. It overwinters, then bolts in spring to reach harvest in early summer, about 2 weeks earlier than modern winter wheat. Harvest when the grain is in the hard dough stage — the straw turns yellow but the heads have not yet shattered — by cutting and binding into sheaves left to dry for a week before threshing. Einkorn retains a tough hull after threshing that requires dehulling before milling; the flour is notably nutty and golden from higher carotenoid content.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before the first fall frost; spelt establishes before winter dormancy and resumes vigorous growth in early spring.

directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 80–100 lb per acre (approximately 2–3 oz per 100 sq ft) in fall to 1 in depth; spelt is taller than modern wheat and slightly more susceptible to lodging in rich soils, so avoid high-nitrogen amendments. It tolerates clay and compacted soils better than most small grains and has reasonable resistance to rust diseases. Harvest at hard dough stage in early summer by cutting, sheaving, and drying for a week before threshing; spelt retains a tough hull (spikelet) that requires a dedicated pearler or dehuller before milling. The resulting flour has a mild, nutty flavor and is widely used by those who tolerate ancient wheats better than modern varieties, though it does contain gluten.

planting.note: Sow in early spring up to the last frost date; flax needs cool conditions to germinate and does not tolerate summer heat.

directions: Broadcast seed at 25–30 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 40 sq ft) and rake to 1/4 in depth on a firm, weed-free seedbed; flax produces delicate sky-blue flowers that last only a morning and then forms round seed capsules (bolls) that rattle when dry. Harvest entire plants when half the bolls have turned brown and the stems are yellowing, cutting at the base and bundling into sheaves; finish drying upside-down for 10–14 days over a tarp. Thresh by beating sheaves inside a barrel, then winnow and screen; the small, oval, brown or golden seeds store for 1–2 years and are rich in omega-3 fatty acids. Grind seeds just before use for maximum nutrition, as whole seeds pass largely undigested.

planting.note: Sow after last frost; chia is a short-day plant that flowers in fall, so it is best suited to climates with a long frost-free autumn.

directions: Surface-sow seed on a firm, moist seedbed and barely press in, as chia seed is mucilaginous and will stick to the surface when wet; thin plants to 18–24 in once seedlings emerge. Chia does not respond well to transplanting and should be direct-sown where it will grow. The plant flowers in autumn as day length shortens and produces whorled spikes of small purple flowers followed by seed-filled calyces; harvest stalks when the calyces begin to dry and before birds consume the seed. Place harvested stalks head-first into paper bags to finish drying, then shake out seed and screen away debris; seeds are rich in omega-3 fatty acids, calcium, and fiber.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; sesame demands heat for germination and will rot in cool, wet soil.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in rows 18–24 in apart after soil reaches at least 70°F; sesame seed is very small, so mixing with dry sand aids even distribution and prevents clumping. Thin to 6–12 in once plants are 3 in tall and keep well-weeded until the canopy closes. The upright plants produce attractive tubular flowers along the stem that develop into rectangular seed capsules; the key challenge is timing harvest before 'shattering' — when ripe capsules split and scatter seed spontaneously. Cut entire stalks when the bottom capsules begin to yellow, invert plants into large paper bags, and allow to dry for a week indoors; the capsules then split easily and seed falls into the bag when shaken.
